title: Skip on step 3 silently discards user-configured examDate and dailyGoal
description: Step 3's onSkip is wired to complete() (line 58), which only sets hasCompletedOnboarding = true (lines 102–105). The local @State vars examDate (line 19) and dailyGoal (line 20) are never flushed to ProgressStore when the user skips. persistPlanThenAdvance() — the only place that calls progressStore.setExamDate() and progressStore.setDailyGoal() — is only reachable via the CTA. A user who changes the exam date to 60 days from now and bumps the goal to 50, then taps 'Bỏ qua', loses both values. After app restart ProgressStore.examDate returns nil (ExamCountdownCard shows the 'Đặt ngày thi' prompt instead of the countdown) and dailyGoal falls back to the UserDefaults integer default of 0, which the store normalises to 30 (ProgressStore+ExamDate.swift line 34). licenseType is unaffected because it is an @AppStorage binding written on every tap.
repro: 1. Install fresh. 2. Advance to step 3 (Thiết lập lộ trình ôn thi). 3. Change the exam date (open the date picker and pick 60 days out). 4. Change the daily goal to 50. 5. Tap 'Bỏ qua'. 6. App opens main content. 7. ExamCountdownCard shows 'Đặt ngày thi' (no date set), and any feature that reads progressStore.dailyGoal or progressStore.examDate gets the fallback defaults instead of the user's choices.

verdict.reasoning:
The code confirms the bug with no ambiguity.

OnboardingView.swift line 58 wires step 3's onSkip to complete(). complete() (lines 102–105) only sets hasCompletedOnboarding = true via @AppStorage. Neither progressStore.setExamDate() nor progressStore.setDailyGoal() is called on this path.

persistPlanThenAdvance() (lines 82–100) is the sole call site for both store writes, and it is reachable only via onContinue — the CTA button. OnboardingSteps.swift confirms that OnboardingStudyPlanStep itself performs no store writes; it operates exclusively on the @Binding vars passed in from OnboardingView's local @State (examDate line 19, dailyGoal line 20).

ProgressStore+ExamDate.swift confirms the downstream consequences exactly as claimed: examDate returns nil when the UserDefaults key is absent (lines 9–18), and dailyGoal normalises a missing/zero key to 30 (lines 33–34) — not the user-selected value.

licenseType is @AppStorage (OnboardingView line 11) so it is written on every tap and is unaffected by skipping, consistent with the claim.

There are no guards, no secondary write paths, and no design rationale that would intentionally discard user-configured values on skip. The bug is real and fully reproducible by following the stated repro steps.

title: daysUntilExam uses raw Date() instead of startOfDay, giving wrong count near midnight
description: ProgressStore.daysUntilExam computes dateComponents([.day], from: Date(), to: examDate).day. Because from: is the current wall-clock moment (not the start of the current day), dateComponents counts only whole 24-hour periods. If it is 11:55 PM and the exam is stored at midnight of the following day (~5 minutes away), the result is 0 instead of the correct 1. The onboarding step-3 view avoids this by using cal.startOfDay(for: Date()) and cal.startOfDay(for: examDate) in its local computeDaysUntilExam helper (OnboardingSteps.swift lines 245–249). The inconsistency means ExamCountdownCard (ExamCountdownCard.swift line 41) and HomeTab (HomeTab.swift line 181) show a day count that is 1 less than expected for most of exam-day's eve, and the daysLeft <= 7 amber warning style (ExamCountdownCard.swift line 43) fires a full day earlier than intended.
repro: 1. Set exam date to tomorrow. 2. At 11:55 PM on the day before the exam, open the Home tab. 3. ExamCountdownCard displays '0 ngày còn lại' in amber warning colour. The onboarding step-3 date picker sub-label would have shown '1 ngày nữa' for the same date using its own startOfDay calculation.

title: pendingExam is never cleared by popToRoot, causing a stale pending exam to be presented after an explicit 'go home' action
description: The onDismiss handler for the fullScreenCover (lines 60-64) unconditionally presents pendingExam when the cover is dismissed, regardless of why the cover was dismissed. popToRoot (line 69) only sets activeExam = nil, which triggers the same onDismiss. If pendingExam was set before popToRoot fires (e.g., the user tapped 'Thi lại' in ExamResultView, which calls the inner openExam setting pendingExam = .mockExam(...) and activeExam = nil), then quickly taps the toolbar 'Hoàn thành' checkmark that also calls popToRoot(), both paths call activeExam = nil. The single onDismiss that fires sees pendingExam != nil and re-presents the mock exam, overriding the user's intent to return to root. The result is: the user taps 'Về trang chủ' but is launched back into a new exam instead.
repro: 1. Open any exam (e.g., MockExam) from ExamTab. 2. Complete the exam so ExamResultView appears. 3. Tap 'Thi lại' button — this calls inner openExam(.mockExam(...)) which sets pendingExam and activeExam = nil, starting cover dismiss. 4. Before the dismiss animation completes, tap the toolbar checkmark ('Hoàn thành') which calls popToRoot() setting activeExam = nil (already nil). 5. onDismiss fires, sees pendingExam != nil, presents the mock exam instead of returning home.
fix:
Add a flag to distinguish 'chain to next exam' dismissal from 'pop to root' dismissal. The simplest fix is for popToRoot to also clear pendingExam:

```swift
.environment(\.popToRoot) {
    pendingExam = nil   // prevent onDismiss from re-presenting
    activeExam = nil
}
```

Alternatively, use a separate `Bool` like `private var shouldDismissToRoot = false`, set it in popToRoot, and check it in onDismiss before re-presenting pendingExam.

verdict.reasoning: The bug is confirmed by the code. In HomeView.swift the `popToRoot` closure (line 69) only sets `activeExam = nil` — it never touches `pendingExam`. The `openExam` inner closure (lines 70-73) sets `pendingExam = newScreen` then `activeExam = nil`, beginning cover dismissal. After that, any call to `popToRoot()` (from "Về trang chủ" or the "Hoàn thành" toolbar button) sets `activeExam = nil` again — a no-op, since it is already nil — while leaving `pendingExam` populated. When the single `onDismiss` handler fires, lines 61-63 are unconditional: `if let next = pendingExam { pendingExam = nil; Task { self.activeExam = next } }`. There is no distinction between a dismiss caused by a pending-exam swap and a dismiss caused by popToRoot. Since `pendingExam != nil`, the new exam is presented regardless of the user's explicit "go home" intent. The scenario is realistic: the dismiss animation takes roughly 0.3-0.4 s, during which the buttons inside the cover remain interactive on iOS — a user tapping "Thi lại" and then immediately tapping "Về trang chủ" (or the checkmark) before the animation completes will trigger this path. No existing guard, default, or nil-check prevents it.

title: iPad NavigationSplitView reuses a single NavigationStack for all tabs, causing stale pushed views to persist across sidebar selection changes
description: On iPad, `iPadLayout` places a single `NavigationStack { tabRoot }` in the detail pane (lines 123-128). When the user changes `selectedTab`, `tabRoot` switches the root content (HomeTab → ExamTab etc.), but the NavigationStack identity does not change. SwiftUI keeps the pushed view stack alive on the same NavigationStack. If the user navigates via a NavigationLink in HomeTab (e.g., to WrongAnswersView, BookmarksView, QuestionSearchView, or SettingsView), then taps a different sidebar entry, the NavigationStack's back-stack still contains the old pushed views. The result: after switching tabs, tapping the system Back button returns to a view from the previous tab; or in some cases the detail column shows the wrong content because the pushed stack occludes the new root.
repro: 1. On iPad, open the app and go to Home tab. 2. Tap 'Câu sai cần ôn' → navigates (NavigationLink) to WrongAnswersView. 3. Tap 'Luyện tập' in the sidebar. 4. The ExamTab root is shown but the navigation bar has a back button. 5. Tap back: returns to WrongAnswersView from the Home tab — wrong tab's content appears in the Luyện tập detail pane.

verdict.reasoning: The code at HomeView.swift lines 123-128 places a single NavigationStack { tabRoot } in the iPad detail pane. The NavigationStack has no path: binding, no .id(selectedTab) modifier, and there is no onChange(of: selectedTab) handler anywhere in the file that would clear the push stack. HomeTab contains multiple NavigationLink(destination:) calls (lines 93, 98, 421, 438, 641, 652, 659) that push onto this shared stack. When selectedTab changes, SwiftUI updates only the root content inside the NavigationStack (tabRoot switches via @ViewBuilder), but the NavigationStack's internal back-stack survives because the view's identity is unchanged. Pushed views from HomeTab therefore persist when the sidebar selection changes to a different tab, causing the wrong-tab content to occlude the new root and the Back button to return to a view from the previous tab. The iPhone layout (lines 85-96) correctly gives each tab its own NavigationStack, so the bug is iPad-only. There are no existing guards, path resets, or structural constraints that would prevent this from triggering.

title: Simultaneous dismissal of two nested fullScreenCovers when 'Luyện câu sai' is tapped from QuestionView's result sheet
description: QuestionView is presented inside HomeView's fullScreenCover(item: $activeExam) (HomeView.swift line 60). QuestionView itself presents a second fullScreenCover(isPresented: $showResultSheet) for its study result sheet (QuestionView.swift line 291). When the user taps 'Luyện X câu sai' (line 351-354), the code executes: showResultSheet = false (dismisses inner cover) and then immediately calls openExam(...) which — via the inner environment set in HomeView line 70-73 — sets pendingExam = newScreen and activeExam = nil (dismisses outer cover). Both covers are dismissed concurrently in the same synchronous call. iOS/SwiftUI does not support dismissing two stacked fullScreenCovers simultaneously on the same UIViewController chain. In practice this causes the outer cover's onDismiss to fire before the inner cover has fully dismissed, leading to the pending exam being presented before the inner cover animation completes, resulting in a visual glitch (black flash or the new exam appearing on top of the partially-dismissed result sheet) or, in some iOS versions, the new cover failing to present at all.
repro: 1. Open QuestionView for any topic (e.g., tap 'Ôn tập chủ đề này'). 2. Answer some questions incorrectly so wrongCount > 0. 3. Reach the end (all questions answered). 4. The result sheet appears (showResultSheet = true). 5. Tap 'Luyện X câu sai'. 6. Both covers dismiss simultaneously — observe a visual glitch or the new QuestionView failing to present.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is real. Here is the exact mechanism:

QuestionView runs inside HomeView's fullScreenCover(item: $activeExam). Inside that cover, HomeView injects a scoped openExam closure (HomeView.swift lines 70-73): `{ newScreen in pendingExam = newScreen; activeExam = nil }`. QuestionView also owns a second fullScreenCover(isPresented: $showResultSheet) at line 291 of QuestionView.swift, stacked on top of the outer cover.

When the user taps 'Luyện X câu sai' (lines 351-354), in the same synchronous button closure:
1. `showResultSheet = false` — begins dismissing the inner fullScreenCover.
2. `openExam(...)` fires immediately — executes `pendingExam = newScreen` then `activeExam = nil` — beginning dismissal of the outer fullScreenCover.

Both state mutations happen in a single synchronous SwiftUI render pass. SwiftUI dispatches two concurrent UIViewController dismissals in the same UIViewController presentation chain. iOS does not support two simultaneous dismissals of stacked fullScreenCovers. The outer cover's onDismiss fires (because activeExam becomes nil), which schedules `Task { @MainActor in self.activeExam = next }` to present the new exam. But at the time onDismiss fires, the inner cover has not yet finished its dismissal animation. The new exam then attempts to present on a partially-dismissed UIViewController hierarchy.

No existing guard prevents this. There is no delay, sequencing, or animation completion callback between the two dismissals. The `Task` in onDismiss only defers re-setting activeExam (the next presentation) — it does not prevent the simultaneous dismissal from occurring in the first place.

The result in practice: a visual glitch (black flash, or the new QuestionView appearing on top of the partially-dismissed result sheet), or in some iOS versions the new cover silently failing to present (UIKit logs a warning and drops the presentation request). The relay mechanism via pendingExam + onDismiss is correctly designed for the normal case where only the outer cover needs to be swapped, but it breaks when it must also wait for an inner cover to finish dismissing first.

title: avgScore can exceed stats.total after license-type switch
description: examStats computes `total = history.first?.totalQuestions ?? 35` (the most recent exam's question count) and `avgScore = sum(score) / count` across ALL history entries regardless of their totalQuestions. When the user switches from B2 (35-question exams) to B1 (30-question exams), old history entries carry score values up to 35 while `stats.total` is now 30. The resulting display `Text("/ \(stats.total) điểm")` shows e.g. '31 / 30 điểm' — an average score that numerically exceeds the stated maximum, which is logically impossible.
repro: 1. Start as B2. Complete at least one exam scoring ≥ 30 (e.g. 33/35). 2. Go to Settings and change license type to B1. 3. Complete one B1 exam (any score, e.g. 28/30). 4. Return to the Home tab. The 'KẾT QUẢ THI THỬ' hero row shows average score greater than the '/ 30 điểm' maximum.
fix:
Average each exam's accuracy fraction (score/totalQuestions) and scale to the current total, so mixed-type history is always normalized:
```swift
let total = history.first?.totalQuestions ?? LicenseType.current.questionsPerExam
let avgScore = history.map { Double($0.score) / Double($0.totalQuestions) * Double(total) }.reduce(0, +) / Double(history.count)
```
This normalizes every historical score to the current exam size before averaging.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is confirmed by reading the code directly. The root cause is a mismatch between two quantities computed from heterogeneous exam history:

1. `stats.total` (line 168) = `history.first?.totalQuestions ?? 35` — always reflects the most-recent exam's question count. After a B2→B1 switch and one B1 exam, this becomes 30.

2. `stats.avgScore` (line 170) = `sum(score) / count` across ALL history entries — no filtering by license type. Because `ExamResult.score` is a raw correct-answer integer (not a percentage), old B2 scores of up to 35 are included in the average.

There is no guard in `examHistory` (ProgressStore lines 156–162), `examStats` (HomeTab lines 163–175), or anywhere else that filters history by license type or prevents avgScore from exceeding stats.total. The history list is shared, unpartitioned storage.

Concrete repro arithmetic: one B2 result of 33/35, then one B1 result of 28/30 → avgScore = (33+28)/2 = 30.5, rounded to 31, displayed as "31 / 30 điểm". That is numerically impossible and will be shown to the user. The condition requires only two exams under different license types, which is an ordinary user action.

title: Empty-history guard hardcodes 35 as the exam total for all license types
description: When `history.isEmpty`, `examStats` returns a tuple with a hardcoded `35` as the `total` field: `return (35, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)`. The denominator label `Text("/ \(stats.total) điểm")` at line 227 is rendered unconditionally (not gated on `hasHistory`), so a B1 user who has not yet taken a mock exam sees '— / 35 điểm' instead of the correct '— / 30 điểm'.
repro: 1. Install fresh or clear exam history. 2. Select B1 as the license type. 3. Go to the Home tab. The 'KẾT QUẢ THI THỬ' card shows '— / 35 điểm' instead of '— / 30 điểm'.

verdict.reasoning: The bug is confirmed real. In HomeTab.swift at line 166, the empty-history guard returns a hardcoded `total` of 35: `return (35, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)`. At line 227, `Text("/ \(stats.total) điểm")` is rendered unconditionally — it is NOT gated on `hasHistory` (only line 223's numeric score is gated). LicenseType.swift confirms B1 has `questionsPerExam = 30` and B2 has `questionsPerExam = 35`. ExamResult.swift line 121 shows that when an exam IS taken, `totalQuestions: questions.count` stores the correct value, so the non-empty path (line 168) correctly reads the actual count. However, on a fresh install or cleared state with B1 selected, `history.isEmpty` is true and the hardcoded 35 is fed to `stats.total`, producing "— / 35 điểm" instead of "— / 30 điểm". The default license type is B2 (hardcoded fallback in `LicenseType.current`), so the bug is invisible for B2 users but is a genuine display error for any B1 user who has not yet taken a mock exam.

title: greetingText and dateString go stale across midnight and app-foreground returns
description: `greetingText` and `dateString` are `@State` properties initialised once at first render (lines 10–11) and refreshed only inside `.onAppear` (lines 68–71). In the iPhone layout, `HomeTab` lives inside a `TabView` (`HomeView.swift` line 85) whose tabs stay resident after their first load. Switching to another tab and back does **not** re-trigger `.onAppear`, so the values freeze. The app's `scenePhase` handler in `GPLX2026App.swift` (line 172) calls only `syncReminders()` — it does not refresh these strings. After midnight the date chip shows yesterday's date; across a greeting-period boundary the salutation is wrong.
repro: 1. Open the app just before midnight (e.g. 23:58). The date chip shows today's date. 2. Keep the app in the foreground (or background briefly and return) past midnight. 3. The Home tab still shows yesterday's date in the calendar chip. Alternately: open the app in the morning, leave it running, come back in the afternoon — the greeting still says 'Chào buổi sáng!'.

verdict.reasoning:
All three failure modes claimed are confirmed by the code.

1. `greetingText` and `dateString` are `@State` properties initialized once at first render (HomeTab.swift lines 10–11). The `.onAppear` block at lines 68–71 does recompute them, but `.onAppear` only fires the **first time** a tab is displayed in iOS 18's resident-tab `TabView`.

2. HomeView.swift (lines 83–96) uses the iOS 18 typed `Tab(...)` API inside `TabView`. These tabs stay resident in memory after first load; switching away and back does NOT re-trigger `.onAppear` on `HomeTab`.

3. GPLX2026App.swift (lines 172–178): the `scenePhase == .active` handler calls only `syncReminders()`. There is no code path that propagates a "refresh date/greeting" signal down to `HomeTab`. `HomeTab` has no `onChange(of: scenePhase)`, no timer, no `NSCalendarDayChanged` observer, and no `.task` that would refresh these strings after initial load.

Result: on iPhone, after the app's Home tab has been shown once, both strings are frozen for the session. A user whose session spans midnight will see yesterday's date in the calendar chip. A user whose session spans a greeting-period boundary (e.g. morning → afternoon) will still see "Chào buổi sáng!". The iPad layout is NOT affected because `tabRoot` reconstructs the view on sidebar selection change, rebuilding `@State`.

No existing guard, default, or call-site constraint prevents this. The bug is genuine and reproducible in normal use.

title: Yêu thích filter shows all topics instead of bookmarked-question topics
description: When the user selects the 'Yêu thích' chip (selectedTypeFilter == .yeuThich), the `questionSection` is shown (line 283) but `topicStats` is computed from all topics with no bookmark filtering (line 339: `progressStore.weakTopics(topics: allTopics)`). `applyMasteryFilter` also has no branch for the yeuThich case. The result is that the 'Yêu thích' tab displays every topic — identical to 'Tất cả' with default mastery filter — making the feature entirely non-functional. The user expects to see only topics that contain their bookmarked questions.
repro: 1. Bookmark at least one question from topic 'Kỹ thuật lái xe'. 2. Open the Practice tab. 3. Tap the 'Yêu thích' chip. The list shows all 5 topics, not just 'Kỹ thuật lái xe'.
fix:
In `questionSection`, after computing `topicStats`, add a bookmark-based filter when `selectedTypeFilter == .yeuThich`. The bookmarked question numbers are in `progressStore.bookmarks`. Compute which topic keys contain at least one bookmarked question, then restrict `topicStats` to those topics before passing to `applyMasteryFilter`:
```swift
let bookmarkKeys: Set<String> = selectedTypeFilter == .yeuThich
    ? Set(questionStore.allQuestions
        .filter { progressStore.bookmarks.contains($0.no) }
        .map { Topic.keyForTopicId($0.topic) })
    : []
let filteredByType = selectedTypeFilter == .yeuThich
    ? topicStats.filter { bookmarkKeys.contains($0.topic.key) }
    : topicStats
let filteredStats = applyMasteryFilter(filteredByType)
```

verdict.reasoning: The bug is real and unambiguous. In PracticeTab.swift, when selectedTypeFilter == .yeuThich, the questionSection computed property runs without any bookmark-based filtering. Line 338 assigns allTopics = questionStore.topics (the full topic list), and line 339 passes it straight into progressStore.weakTopics(topics:), which in ProgressStore+Analytics.swift (line 28–37) simply maps every topic to its progress stats with no awareness of bookmarks. There is no conditional branch inside questionSection that keys on selectedTypeFilter == .yeuThich to narrow allTopics to only those whose question IDs intersect progressStore.bookmarks. The applyMasteryFilter function only switches on selectedMasteryFilter (which stays at .tatCa because the MasteryFilterBar is hidden at line 354 for .yeuThich), so it returns all stats unchanged. No helper method exists anywhere in the codebase (confirmed by grep) that maps bookmark question IDs back to their parent topics. The only .yeuThich-specific behavior that exists is: hiding the mastery sub-filter bar (line 354) and showing a different empty-state message (line 368). The topic list itself is identical to what .tatCa renders. The repro scenario described is accurate: selecting Yêu thích shows all topics, not just those containing bookmarked questions.

title: Hero card 'Continue learning' always starts from question 0, ignoring saved position
description: `heroCard` constructs the hero card with `eyebrow = 'TIẾP TỤC HỌC'` when `resumeTopic != nil` (i.e., the user has a saved position), but the play button calls `openExam(.questionView(topicKey: heroKey, startIndex: 0))` — hardcoding `startIndex: 0`. The saved position is stored in `progressStore.lastQuestionIndex` (set by `saveLastPosition`) but is never read here. Every 'Continue' tap restarts from the first question in the topic.
repro: 1. Study topic 'Biển báo' and stop at question 15. 2. Background the app. 3. Return to the Practice tab. 4. Tap the hero card play button. The session opens at question 1 instead of question 15.
fix:
Pass the saved index when a resume topic exists:
```swift
let startIndex = resumeTopic != nil ? progressStore.lastQuestionIndex : 0
// ...
openExam(.questionView(topicKey: heroKey, startIndex: startIndex))
```

verdict.reasoning: The bug is confirmed by direct code inspection. PracticeTab.swift line 330 calls openExam(.questionView(topicKey: heroKey, startIndex: 0)) with a hardcoded 0, even when resumeTopic != nil (meaning a saved position exists). progressStore.lastQuestionIndex holds the real saved index (written by saveLastPosition in QuestionView.swift lines 295 and 536), but it is never read in heroCard. QuestionView.init sets _currentIndex = State(initialValue: startIndex), so passing 0 unconditionally starts from the first question. HomeTab.swift line 580 shows the correct pattern: startIndex: progressStore.lastQuestionIndex — its "Tiếp tục" tile does the right thing while PracticeTab's hero card does not. No guard, default, or call-site constraint prevents the wrong behavior from manifesting. The repro scenario will produce exactly the described outcome.

title: Topic accuracy displayed to user uses wrong denominator (total questions vs attempted questions)
description: In `questionSection`, the `accuracy` value passed to `TopicRowCard` is `Double(item.correct) / Double(item.total)` (line 376), where `item.total` is the full question count of the topic (e.g. 600 for topic 1). However, the mastery filter thresholds in `applyMasteryFilter` use `item.accuracy` which is `correct/attempted` (from `weakTopics()`). This mismatch causes two problems: (1) a user who answered 4/5 attempted questions correctly (80% accuracy, classified as 'Đã thuộc') sees '4/600 = 0%' in the topic card instead of '80%'; (2) a topic genuinely requiring more study can appear in 'Đã thuộc' while displaying a near-zero accuracy, confusing the user.
repro: 1. Answer 5 questions in any topic, getting 4 right. 2. On the Practice tab, tap 'Đã thuộc' (mastery filter). 3. The topic appears (because 4/5 = 80%), but the topic card accuracy pill reads '4/[totalCount] * 100 %' rather than '80%'.
fix:
Use `item.accuracy` (already correctly computed as `correct/attempted`) for the display:
```swift
let accuracy: Double? = item.attempted > 0 ? item.accuracy : nil
```

verdict.reasoning: The bug is confirmed by direct code inspection. In `weakTopics()` (ProgressStore+Analytics.swift line 33), `accuracy` is computed as `Double(correct) / Double(attempted)` — correct over attempted questions. `applyMasteryFilter` (PracticeTab.swift lines 402–408) correctly uses `$0.accuracy` (correct/attempted) to bucket topics into daThuoc/chuaThuoc/dangOn. However, the display expression at lines 375–376 computes `Double(item.correct) / Double(item.total)` — dividing by `item.total`, which is `topic.questionCount` (the full question-bank size, e.g. 600), not `item.attempted`. These two denominators are only equal when the user has answered every single question in the topic. In the normal case (partial attempt), they diverge severely: a user who answered 4 of 5 questions correctly gets classified correctly into 'Đã thuộc' (4/5 = 80% ≥ 0.8) but the displayed accuracy pill shows 4/600 ≈ 0.67%, making it appear as near-zero. The fix is trivial: change line 376 to `Double(item.attempted)` instead of `Double(item.total)`. Both stated problems in the bug report (wrong display value, and the confusion it causes) are real and reproducible any time a user has not attempted every question in a topic.

title: chapterAverageScore inflates with repeated sessions — same situation counted multiple times per history entry
description: `chapterAverageScore(chapterId:)` iterates all `hazardHistory` entries and accumulates score for every detail whose `situationId` falls in the chapter range, including duplicates across exam runs. Because each 10-situation exam picks randomly from all 120, a situation from chapter 1 may appear in many history entries. This means a chapter where the user consistently scores 5/5 on one situation but 0/5 on all others can appear to have a high average because the single perfect situation inflates `totalScore` relative to `totalMax`. More practically: repeating an exam many times with poor scores on chapter situations will lower the displayed chapter score monotonically, even if recent performance is improving — this is cumulative-average behavior, not recent-average.
repro: 1. Complete 10 hazard exams. In each exam, situation 1 (chapter 1) appears and the user scores 0. 2. Then the user practices deliberately and scores 5 on situation 1. 3. `chapterAverageScore(1)` returns approximately 5/(11*5) = 9%, not 100%.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is real and confirmed by the code.

`chapterAverageScore(chapterId:)` in /Users/maitrungkien/Desktop/project/GPLX2026/GPLX2026/Core/Storage/ProgressStore+ExamHistory.swift (lines 42–55) accumulates totalScore and totalMax over every SituationDetail in every HazardResult in hazardHistory where the situationId falls in the chapter's range. It does not deduplicate by situationId.

`HazardSituation.random(count:)` (line 318 of HazardSituation.swift) is implemented as `Array(all.shuffled().prefix(count))` — a fully random draw with no cross-session deduplication — meaning the same situationId can and will appear in multiple exam sessions. Each appearance contributes independently to totalScore and totalMax.

The repro scenario is arithmetically correct: 10 sessions where situation 1 scores 0 followed by 1 session where it scores 5 yields totalScore=5, totalMax=55, result=9.1%, not 100%. There is no guard, cache invalidation, deduplication, or recency weighting anywhere in the function or at its call sites (MoPhongTab.swift lines 163 and 253–258) that would prevent or correct this.

The UI presents this value as a chapter mastery/readiness score (threshold 0.8 drives a warning badge), so the distortion from random repetition frequency directly produces a misleading display of user competency. The behavior is cumulative-historical, not per-situation average or recency-weighted, and is not documented anywhere as intentional.

title: handleSimulationTimeout overwrites a confirmed correct answer when timer fires concurrently with user confirmation
description: handleSimulationTimeout() has no guard against isRevealed, so it can run AFTER confirmSimulationAnswer() has already committed the user's answer. confirmSimulationAnswer() sets isRevealed = true and calls timer?.invalidate(), but timer?.invalidate() only stops future firings — it does NOT cancel a Task { @MainActor in ... } that was already enqueued by the timer callback. That queued task calls handleSimulationTimeout(), which unconditionally overwrites answers[currentIndex] = -1 (the timeout sentinel), records the question as wrong in progressStore, and sets timePerScenario[currentIndex] = 60. The final score and progress data for this question are corrupted: a correctly-answered question is counted as a timeout/wrong answer.
repro: 1. Run a simulation exam. 2. On any question, wait until the countdown reaches 1 second. 3. Select an answer and tap 'Xác nhận' at the exact moment the timer reaches 0. 4. confirmSimulationAnswer runs synchronously (UIKit touch event); the already-enqueued timer Task also executes immediately after on the MainActor queue. 5. The result view shows the question as timed-out wrong even though the user submitted a correct answer. The race window is approximately 1 second per question.
fix:
Add guard !isRevealed else { return } at the start of handleSimulationTimeout(), mirroring the existing guard in confirmSimulationAnswer():

private func handleSimulationTimeout() {
    guard !isRevealed else { return }   // ← add this
    timer?.invalidate()
    answers[currentIndex] = -1
    timePerScenario[currentIndex] = AppConstants.Simulation.scenarioTimeSeconds
    Haptics.notification(.warning)
    isRevealed = true
    let question = questions[currentIndex]
    let topicKey = Topic.keyForTopicId(question.topic)
    progressStore.recordQuestionAnswer(topicKey: topicKey, questionNo: question.no, correct: false)
}

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is real and exploitable. Here is the exact mechanism:

1. The scenario timer is added to RunLoop.main with .common mode (line 575). Its callback fires on the main thread.

2. Inside that callback, `Task { @MainActor in ... }` enqueues work onto the main actor's serial queue. When remaining <= 0, that task will call handleSimulationTimeout().

3. confirmSimulationAnswer() (line 585) has a guard !isRevealed else { return } at the top. It sets isRevealed = true, records the correct answer via progressStore, and calls timer?.invalidate() (line 592).

4. handleSimulationTimeout() (line 601) has NO guard against isRevealed. It unconditionally overwrites answers[currentIndex] = -1 (the timeout sentinel, line 604), sets timePerScenario[currentIndex] = scenarioTimeSeconds (line 605), and calls progressStore.recordQuestionAnswer(..., correct: false) (line 612).

The race: when the timer fires at remaining == 0, it enqueues the Task on the main actor. If the user's tap confirmation runs (also on the main actor) before that Task gets a chance to run — which is plausible because the task is asynchronously scheduled — confirmSimulationAnswer commits the correct answer. Then the already-enqueued task resumes and handleSimulationTimeout() runs unconditionally, overwriting the committed correct answer with -1 and recording it as wrong.

timer?.invalidate() called in confirmSimulationAnswer() does NOT cancel a Task that was already created by the timer callback before invalidate() was called. The Task is already in the main actor queue and will run.

The fix would be to add `guard !isRevealed else { return }` at the top of handleSimulationTimeout(), mirroring the guard already present in confirmSimulationAnswer() and handleSimulationAnswerSelection().

title: ExamResult.calculate uses full-license pass threshold for daily challenges, making result.passed always false
description: ExamResult.calculate() uses LicenseType.current.passThreshold unconditionally: 'let passed = correctCount >= LicenseType.current.passThreshold && wrongDiemLietCount == 0'. For B2 this threshold is 32; for B1 it is 26. A daily challenge has exactly 10 questions (AppConstants.DailyChallenge.questionsCount), so correctCount can be at most 10. 10 >= 32 (or 10 >= 26) is always false, meaning result.passed is always false for every daily challenge regardless of the user's performance. Downstream code in DailyChallengeResultView and ExamTab works around this by hardcoding isPassed: true and passed: { _ in true }, but those workarounds then display ALL daily challenges — including genuinely poor results — as passed.
repro: 1. Complete a daily challenge with fewer than 5 correct answers. 2. Check the stored ExamResult.passed value — it is false (as expected for a bad result). 3. The result view shows 'ĐẠT' and the history list shows a green checkmark because the views ignore result.passed entirely. Alternatively: complete a daily challenge getting 9/10 correct — the UI still shows the same 'passed' badge because the pass/fail logic is bypassed.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is real and confirmed by the code. Three distinct defects exist and interact:

1. ExamResult.calculate (ExamResult.swift:117) uses LicenseType.current.passThreshold unconditionally. For B2 that is 32, for B1 it is 26. A daily challenge has exactly 10 questions (AppConstants.DailyChallenge.questionsCount = 10), so correctCount is at most 10. The condition '10 >= 32' or '10 >= 26' is always false, so result.passed is always stored as false in the persisted ExamResult for every daily challenge.

2. In BaseExamView.submitDailyChallenge(), the haptic at line 555 reads 'Haptics.notification(result.passed ? .success : .warning)'. Since result.passed is always false, the app always fires the warning haptic on daily challenge completion — a user-observable wrong behavior regardless of how well the user performed.

3. The two downstream views use hardcoded workarounds that do eliminate the crash but produce the inverse wrong behavior: DailyChallengeResultView (lines 82 and 91) passes 'isPassed: true' unconditionally to ResultHero, and ExamTab.dailyChallengeHistory (line 668) passes 'passed: { _ in true }' to HistoryList. These workarounds cause EVERY daily challenge — including a 0/10 or 1/10 score — to display as passed ('ĐẠT'/green checkmark) in both the result view and the history list. A user who scores 1 out of 10 sees the same congratulatory result screen and green history badge as someone who scored 10 out of 10.

There are no guards, defaults, or call-site constraints that prevent these defects from triggering. The bug is not by design: the presence of two separate hardcoded workarounds in the UI layer is evidence that the pass/fail logic is known to be broken and was patched superficially rather than fixed at the source. The net observable effect is: (a) the wrong haptic always plays, (b) every daily challenge result is displayed as passed regardless of actual score, making the pass/fail distinction meaningless for daily challenges.

title: DailyChallengeResultView hardcodes isPassed: true, hiding real failure state from the user
description: Both ResultHero calls (iPad layout line 82, iPhone layout line 90) pass isPassed: true unconditionally instead of using result.passed. ResultHero uses isPassed to render the progress ring color (green vs red), the 'ĐẠT'/'TRƯỢT' status badge, and the subtitle text is also hardcoded to 'Thử thách hôm nay hoàn thành!' regardless of outcome. A user who scores 1/10 sees the same green 'ĐẠT' ring and success message as one who scores 10/10. This is a consequence of the root bug in ExamResult.calculate (filed separately), but even after that is fixed the hardcoding here would need to change.
repro: 1. Complete a daily challenge answering all questions wrong. 2. The result view shows a green progress ring, 'ĐẠT' badge, and 'Thử thách hôm nay hoàn thành!' text. No failure feedback is given.
fix:
Replace the hardcoded isPassed: true with result.passed, and update the subtitle to reflect the actual outcome:

ResultHero(
    isPassed: result.passed,   // ← was: true
    score: correctCount,
    total: questions.count,
    subtitle: result.passed
        ? "Thử thách hôm nay hoàn thành!"
        : "Cố gắng hơn vào ngày mai nhé!"
)

Apply the same change to both the iPad and iPhone branches.

verdict.reasoning: The hardcoding is confirmed in DailyChallengeResultView.swift at lines 82 and 90: both ResultHero calls pass isPassed: true unconditionally. ResultHero uses isPassed to drive the ring color (statusColor = appSuccess vs appError), the status badge text ("ĐẠT" vs "TRƯỢT"), and the subtitle is also hardcoded. The result parameter carries a real ExamResult whose passed field is computed by ExamResult.calculate() (ExamResult.swift line 117) as correctCount >= LicenseType.current.passThreshold && wrongDiemLietCount == 0 — it is fully capable of being false. The call site in BaseExamView.swift line 546 stores that computed result, and line 555 even branches on result.passed for haptic feedback, confirming the runtime knows the correct pass/fail state but DailyChallengeResultView ignores it. No guard, default, or design constraint forces all daily challenge results to be passing. A user who answers every question wrong will score 0 and result.passed will be false, yet they will see a green ring and "ĐẠT" badge. The normal ExamResultView correctly uses examResult.passed throughout, making this an isolated regression in the daily challenge path.

title: Daily challenge history always shows green 'Đạt' badge regardless of actual result
description: In dailyChallengeHistory, HistoryList is called with passed: { _ in true }. HistoryRow uses this value to render either a green checkmark + 'Đạt' badge or a red X + 'Trượt' badge. Because the closure always returns true, every entry in the daily challenge history list shows as passed with a green icon, even for failed attempts. This is a second display site affected by the root ExamResult pass-threshold bug.
repro: 1. Complete a daily challenge (any score). 2. Navigate to the Exam tab and scroll to 'Lịch sử thử thách'. 3. Every history row shows a green checkmark and 'Đạt' label, regardless of how many questions were answered correctly. Once the root ExamResult.calculate bug is fixed with a proper daily challenge threshold, this line will cause failed sessions to still appear as passed.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is confirmed and unambiguous. At ExamTab.swift:668, `HistoryList` for daily challenge history is called with `passed: { _ in true }`, a constant closure that always returns `true`. `HistoryRow` uses this value directly to choose the icon (`checkmark.circle.fill` vs `xmark.circle.fill`) and badge text (`"Đạt"` vs `"Trượt"`), so every daily challenge history row renders green with "Đạt" regardless of actual result.

The stored `ExamResult.passed` field is correctly computed by `ExamResult.calculate()` (using the license pass threshold and diemLiet check) and saved to disk via `progressStore.recordDailyChallengeResult()`. The submit path even fires `Haptics.notification(result.passed ? .success : .warning)`, confirming failures are an expected outcome. But the history display ignores the stored field entirely.

The analogous `hazardHistory` section at line 652 correctly uses `passed: \.passed` (a key-path to the actual stored field), making the divergence at line 668 clearly unintentional rather than by design. There are no guards or invariants that prevent a daily challenge from being failed and stored with `passed = false`, so the wrong-badge condition can and will occur for any user who fails a daily challenge.

title: Timed-out simulation questions shown as .wrong (red) instead of .unanswered (gray)
description: SimulationResultView receives the raw answers: [Int: Int] dict from BaseExamView, which stores -1 as a sentinel for timed-out questions. At line 99, `let selectedId = answers[index]` returns Optional(-1) for timed-out slots. Line 103 evaluates `selectedId == nil` — which is false because Optional(-1) is not nil — so the status resolves to .wrong (red X) instead of .unanswered (gray dash). The 'Hết giờ' time-out badge on line 105 is also never shown for the same reason. The inconsistency is evident by comparing the live result with the history view: SimulationHistoryDetailView reconstructs answers from scenarioDetails where timed-out entries have nil selectedAnswerId, so history correctly shows .unanswered.
repro: Start a simulation exam. Let the 60-second timer expire on any question without selecting an answer. Complete the remaining questions and finish. In the result screen, the timed-out question appears with a red X (wrong) rather than a gray dash (unanswered), and shows no 'Hết giờ' badge. Open the same result from history — it correctly shows the gray dash and no answer highlighted.
fix: In SimulationResultView, treat -1 as no selection. Change line 99 to: `let rawId = answers[index]; let selectedId: Int? = (rawId == nil || rawId == -1) ? nil : rawId`. The rest of the logic (isCorrect, status, timeUsedBadge) then works correctly with a true nil for timed-out questions.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is real and confirmed by a precise code path trace.

In BaseExamView.swift line 604, handleSimulationTimeout() stores answers[currentIndex] = -1 (sentinel for timeout). This raw dict is passed unchanged to SimulationResultView via the resultDestination property (lines 400-405 of BaseExamView.swift).

In SimulationResultView.swift lines 99-105, let selectedId = answers[index] returns Optional(-1) for timed-out slots — not nil. The guard selectedId == nil evaluates to false (Optional(-1) is non-nil), so the branch falls through to: isCorrect ? .correct : .wrong. Since -1 matches no real answer ID, isCorrect is false, giving .wrong (red X). The timeUsedBadge: selectedId == nil ? "Hết giờ" : nil also evaluates to nil for the same reason, so the "Hết giờ" badge is never shown.

The history path is genuinely different: SimulationResult.calculate() in SimulationResult.swift lines 97-107 explicitly detects the sentinel (selectedId == nil || selectedId == -1) and stores selectedAnswerId: isTimedOut ? nil : selectedId in each ScenarioDetail. SimulationHistoryDetailView.swift lines 13-19 reconstructs the answers dict only for non-nil selectedAnswerId values, so timed-out entries are absent from the dict entirely. answers[index] then returns nil (true nil, not Optional(-1)), and the nil check correctly resolves to .unanswered with the "Hết giờ" badge.

There is no guard, default, or call-site constraint that prevents the -1 sentinel from reaching SimulationResultView. The model layer already cleans it up correctly for persistence, but the live in-session path bypasses that cleanup. The discrepancy between live result (red X, no badge) and history (gray dash, no highlighted answer) is exactly as described in the claim.

title: HazardTimelineCard 'Hoàn hảo' (perfect) zone visually covers 50% of the scoring window but the actual score≥4 boundary is only 30% in
description: perfectZoneEnd in HazardTimelineCard is computed as `(perfectStart + perfectEnd) / 2.0 / totalDuration`, placing the green/yellow boundary at the midpoint of the scoring window (50%). However, the scoring formula in HazardSituation.score() gives score≥4 only when fraction <= 0.3 (first 30% of window): score=4 requires round(5*(1-fraction))>=4, i.e., 5*(1-fraction)>=3.5, i.e., fraction<=0.3. The ReactionTimingBar therefore renders the 'Hoàn hảo' green zone 1.67x wider than the zone that actually earns 4+ points. A user whose tap lands in the green visual zone between 30%–50% of the window actually receives score 3 ('Tốt'), not 4 ('Rất tốt'). This directly misleads users about their reaction timing quality.
repro: Complete a hazard situation with a tap at 40% of the way through the scoring window (e.g., perfectStart=10s, perfectEnd=13s → tap at 11.2s, fraction=0.4). The result screen shows the tap marker inside the green 'Hoàn hảo' zone, but the score is 3 ('Tốt'), not 4 ('Rất tốt').
fix: Compute perfectZoneEnd using the actual score-4 boundary (fraction=0.3): `let perfectBoundary = perfectStart + 0.3 * (perfectEnd - perfectStart); return perfectBoundary / totalDuration`. This aligns the visual green zone in ReactionTimingBar with the zone that truly earns score≥4.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is confirmed by direct code inspection and arithmetic.

Scoring formula (HazardSituation.swift:26-31): score = max(0, round(5.0 * (1.0 - fraction))), where fraction = (tapTime - perfectStart) / (perfectEnd - perfectStart). Score >= 4 ("Rất tốt") requires fraction <= 0.30. At fraction=0.31, round(5*0.69) = round(3.45) = 3. At fraction=0.40, round(5*0.60) = round(3.0) = 3. At fraction=0.50 (the visual boundary), round(5*0.50) = round(2.5) = 2.

Visual green zone boundary (HazardResultView.swift:418-422): perfectZoneEnd = (perfectStart + perfectEnd) / 2.0 / totalDuration. This places the green/yellow visual divide at the midpoint of the scoring window, i.e., 50% through the window. The green ("Hoàn hảo") zone on the bar spans from earlyEnd (= perfectStart) to perfectZoneEnd (= 50% of window). But the actual score >= 4 boundary is at 30% of the window.

Verified repro: perfectStart=10, perfectEnd=13 (a typical situation). Tap at 11.2s = 40% of window. Bar fraction = 11.2/16.0 = 0.70, inside green zone (0.625 to 0.719). Actual score = round(5*(1-0.4)) = round(3.0) = 3. The result screen shows the tap marker inside the green "Hoàn hảo" zone, yet the score is 3, labelled "Tốt" in scoreLabelFor() (HazardTestView.swift:630). There are no guards, clamps, or alternative code paths that correct this. The HazardTimelineCard comment ("Perfect = front half of window; Good = back half") makes the intent explicit but it is wrong relative to the scoring math. The discrepancy covers the entire 30%-50% fraction range of every scoring window across all 120 situations.

title: Unconfirmed answer selection is silently cleared when navigating away from a simulation question
description: In simulation mode a user can select an answer tile (sets selectedAnswerId) without confirming (which would require pressing 'Xác nhận' / 'Next'). If they navigate away via the Prev button or the question grid — both of which call saveCurrentTimerState() then change currentIndex — restoreStateForCurrentIndex() is called for the new question. When returning to the original question, restoreStateForCurrentIndex() finds answers[currentIndex]==nil (not yet confirmed) and unconditionally sets selectedAnswerId=nil (line 627), discarding the user's in-progress selection. The timer resumes from where it was (saved correctly), but the selected answer highlight is gone, forcing the user to re-select before they can confirm. This is unexpected because the timer evidence implies their time was preserved but their work was not.
repro: Start a simulation exam. On question 1, tap any answer tile (it highlights but do NOT press 'Xác nhận'). Press the Prev button (or tap question 2 in the grid and come back). The previously highlighted answer is no longer selected.
fix: Save selectedAnswerId alongside the remaining time in saveCurrentTimerState, and restore it in restoreStateForCurrentIndex when re-entering an unconfirmed question. Add `savedSelectedAnswer: [Int: Int] = [:]` state, save with `savedSelectedAnswer[currentIndex] = selectedAnswerId.map { $0 } ?? -1` in saveCurrentTimerState when not revealed, and restore with `selectedAnswerId = savedSelectedAnswer[currentIndex].flatMap { $0 == -1 ? nil : $0 }` in the else branch of restoreStateForCurrentIndex.

verdict.reasoning: The code confirms the bug. In simulation mode, selecting an answer sets `selectedAnswerId` (line 582) but does NOT write to `answers[currentIndex]` — that only happens in `confirmSimulationAnswer` (line 588). When the user navigates away, `saveCurrentTimerState` (lines 615-619) persists only `savedRemainingTime[currentIndex]`; it has no awareness of `selectedAnswerId`. On return, `restoreStateForCurrentIndex` (lines 621-632) checks `answers[currentIndex]`: if nil (unconfirmed), the else-branch unconditionally sets `selectedAnswerId = nil` (line 627), discarding the in-progress selection. Both navigation paths (Prev via `handlePrev` lines 422-428, and grid tap via `onSelectIndex` lines 296-302) call `saveCurrentTimerState` then `restoreStateForCurrentIndex`, so both routes trigger the loss. There is no `savedSelectedAnswer` dictionary or any other mechanism that would preserve an unconfirmed `selectedAnswerId` across index changes. The timer state is restored correctly (the saved time is reloaded) but the answer highlight is silently cleared — exactly matching the described symptom.

title: wifiOnly toggle is a complete no-op — downloads always allowed over cellular
description: The `wifiOnlyDownload` @AppStorage value is stored and toggled in OfflineDownloadView but is never read by HazardVideoCache. All download paths (downloadVideo, downloadChapter, downloadAll) use a URLSession created with URLSessionConfiguration.default, which has allowsCellularAccess = true and no NWPathMonitor/network-type check. The toggle persists a preference that is silently ignored; the user believes they are protected from cellular data usage but videos download over cellular regardless.
repro: 1. Open Quản lý tải offline. 2. Confirm 'Chỉ tải qua Wi-Fi' is ON (default). 3. Disconnect from Wi-Fi (cellular only). 4. Tap 'Tải tất cả' or any per-chapter download arrow. Observe: download starts and proceeds over cellular, violating the user's explicit setting.
fix: In HazardVideoCache, read `UserDefaults.standard.bool(forKey: "wifiOnlyDownload")` at the start of downloadVideo(for:) and guard against it: e.g. `if UserDefaults.standard.bool(forKey: "wifiOnlyDownload") { let path = NWPathMonitor.currentPath; guard path.usesInterfaceType(.wifi) else { failedIds.insert(situation.id); return } }`. Alternatively set `downloadSession`'s URLSessionConfiguration.allowsCellularAccess = !wifiOnlyDownload when the preference changes, by recreating or replacing the session configuration. A reactive approach is to observe the UserDefaults key via a Combine publisher or @MainActor property on HazardVideoCache so the guard is always current.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is real and fully confirmed by static analysis with no ambiguity.

The `wifiOnlyDownload` @AppStorage key is declared and toggled exclusively inside OfflineDownloadView.swift (lines 9, 391, 395, 400, 402, 410). Every reference is either the declaration, the toggle action, or UI rendering (capsule color, alignment, accessibility value). It is never read outside that file.

HazardVideoCache.swift is the sole download engine. Its URLSession is created at line 36 with `URLSessionConfiguration.default`, which has `allowsCellularAccess = true` by default. No property on that configuration is subsequently set to restrict cellular access. There is no NWPathMonitor, no `isExpensive`/`isConstrained` path check, and no guard statement gating any of the three download entry points (`downloadVideo(for:)`, `downloadChapter(_:)`, `downloadAll()`) on network type. The `wifiOnlyDownload` preference is never passed into HazardVideoCache, nor does HazardVideoCache read UserDefaults/AppStorage directly.

Result: the user can set the "Chỉ tải qua Wi-Fi" toggle to ON (the default), disconnect from Wi-Fi, tap any download button, and videos will download over cellular — directly violating the stated preference. The toggle is a complete no-op with respect to actual download behavior.

title: Paused chapter is permanently stuck — no UI path to resume
description: chapterStateButton collapses `isDownloading || isPaused` into one branch that always shows a pause icon and always calls `videoCache.pauseChapter(chapter.id)`. When a chapter has been paused (`isPaused = true, isDownloading = false`), this branch is entered, but tapping the button calls `pauseChapter()` again which just re-inserts into `pausedChapters` (a Set.insert no-op) and cancels already-cancelled tasks. The idle/download branch (showing the download arrow) only renders when both `isDownloading` and `isPaused` are false — a state that is never reached after a pause without a full app restart clearing in-memory state. There is no resume button exposed to the user.
repro: 1. Open Quản lý tải offline. 2. Tap the download arrow for chapter 1. 3. While downloading, tap the pause button. 4. Chapter is now paused. Observe: the pause icon remains; tapping it has no effect; there is no way to resume chapter 1 without restarting the app.
fix: Separate the two states in chapterStateButton. When `isPaused && !isDownloading`, show a resume/download icon and call `Task { await videoCache.downloadChapter(chapter.id) }` on tap (downloadChapter already calls `pausedChapters.remove(chapterId)` at line 129, which resumes correctly). The pause icon and pauseChapter() call should only be shown when `isDownloading` is true.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is confirmed by tracing the exact state machine in the code.

In chapterStateButton (OfflineDownloadView.swift line 324), the condition `isDownloading || isPaused` collapses two conceptually different states into one branch. Both states render a pause icon and wire the tap action to `videoCache.pauseChapter(chapter.id)`.

After a chapter is paused:
- `downloadingChapters` no longer contains the chapter (removed by pauseChapter at line 150)
- `pausedChapters` contains the chapter (inserted at line 149)
- Therefore `isDownloading = false`, `isPaused = true`
- The `else if isDownloading || isPaused` branch is entered — showing the pause icon again
- Tapping it calls `pauseChapter()` again, which is a no-op (Set.insert on an already-present member, cancel on already-cancelled tasks)

The idle/download-arrow branch (`else`) requires both flags to be false, which is impossible while the chapter remains in `pausedChapters`.

The only code paths that clear `pausedChapters` for a specific chapter are:
1. `downloadChapter()` (line 129: `pausedChapters.remove(chapterId)`) — but this is only called from the `else` branch, which is unreachable while `isPaused` is true
2. `cancelAll()` (line 253: `pausedChapters.removeAll()`) — not exposed as a per-chapter UI action
3. `clearCache()` (line 271: `pausedChapters.removeAll()`) — destructive, deletes all downloaded videos

The "Download All" button (`downloadAll()`) explicitly does NOT clear `pausedChapters` — it only clears `isPausedAll`. So using "Tải tất cả" after a per-chapter pause does not unblock the stuck chapter's state button.

An app restart clears the in-memory @Observable state, so `pausedChapters` becomes empty on relaunch, and the chapter shows the download arrow again — confirming the claim that a restart is the only recovery path.

The bug is real: once a chapter is paused via the per-chapter pause button, there is no UI path to resume that specific chapter without restarting the app.

title: clearAllProgress() does not remove exam_date, daily_goal, or study_activity from UserDefaults
description: clearAllProgress() calls clearTopicProgress, clearExamHistory, clearSimulationHistory, clearHazardHistory, clearBookmarks, clearWrongAnswers, and clearStreak. None of these touch the keys 'exam_date', 'daily_goal' (owned by ProgressStore+ExamDate.swift), or 'study_activity' (owned by ProgressStore+Activity.swift). invalidateCaches() resets the in-memory caches for those fields, so immediately after a 'clear all', the next read of examDate/dailyGoal/studyActivity re-hydrates from the stale UserDefaults values. The user taps 'Xoá tất cả dữ liệu', sees the success toast, but their exam date, custom daily goal, and full study-activity calendar persist on disk.
repro: 1. Set a custom exam date (e.g. next month) and daily goal (e.g. 50). 2. Tap 'Xoá tất cả dữ liệu' and confirm. 3. Navigate away and back to Settings. Observe: 'Ngày thi' still shows the previously set date; daily goal stepper still shows 50; the readiness/calendar data in ProgressStore+Analytics still counts historical activity.
fix: Add a clearExamDateAndGoal() helper in ProgressStore+ExamDate.swift that calls safeWrite { $0.removeObject(forKey: Self.examDateKey); $0.removeObject(forKey: Self.dailyGoalKey) } and resets _examDateCacheLoaded/_dailyGoalCache. Add a clearStudyActivity() helper in ProgressStore+Activity.swift similarly. Call both from clearAllProgress().

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is real and verified by direct code inspection.

clearAllProgress() at /Users/maitrungkien/Desktop/project/GPLX2026/GPLX2026/Core/Storage/ProgressStore.swift:456-464 calls seven sub-functions and then invalidateCaches(). None of those seven sub-functions remove the UserDefaults keys "exam_date", "daily_goal", or "study_activity". invalidateCaches() (lines 84-111) does nil out the corresponding in-memory caches (_examDateCache, _examDateCacheLoaded, _dailyGoalCache, _studyActivityCache), but it issues no UserDefaults.removeObject calls for those three keys.

The three keys are defined only in extension files:
- "exam_date" and "daily_goal" are private statics in ProgressStore+ExamDate.swift (lines 5-6), accessed via examDate and dailyGoal computed properties.
- "study_activity" is a private static in ProgressStore+Activity.swift (line 5), accessed via studyActivity.

After clearAllProgress() returns, the in-memory caches are nil. The next read of examDate, dailyGoal, or studyActivity goes through the guard/defaults.object/defaults.integer path and re-hydrates from the stale UserDefaults backing store, because those keys were never removed from disk. The SettingsView at line 369 calls progressStore.clearAllProgress() directly with no supplementary cleanup.

There is no guard, fallback default, or call-site constraint that prevents this. The behaviour the claim describes — exam date, daily goal, and study activity surviving a "Xoá tất cả dữ liệu" reset — is exactly what the code produces.

title: clearAllProgress does not clear study_activity or daily challenge data from UserDefaults
description: clearAllProgress() calls clearTopicProgress, clearExamHistory, clearSimulationHistory, clearHazardHistory, clearBookmarks, clearWrongAnswers, clearStreak, and invalidateCaches. None of these remove the 'study_activity' key (owned by ProgressStore+Activity.swift) or the daily challenge keys: 'daily_challenge_history', 'daily_challenge_last_date', 'daily_challenge_streak' (owned by ProgressStore+Engagement.swift). invalidateCaches() does nil the in-memory caches for these, but because there is no matching safeWrite to remove the UserDefaults keys, the very next read (triggered by SwiftUI re-render) repopulates those caches from the still-present UserDefaults data. Result: after the user taps 'Xoá tất cả dữ liệu', the activity heatmap (ActivityCalendarCard reads progressStore.studyActivity) and the daily challenge streak/history panel (ExamTab reads progressStore.dailyChallengeHistory, .dailyChallengeStreak, .hasCompletedTodayChallenge) continue to display old data as if nothing was cleared.
repro: 1. Answer several questions and complete a daily challenge so heatmap and daily-challenge streak are non-zero. 2. Go to Settings -> 'Xoá tất cả dữ liệu' and confirm. 3. Navigate back to Home / Exam tab. The activity heatmap and daily challenge streak/count still show the old values instead of zero.

verdict.reasoning: clearAllProgress() (ProgressStore.swift:456-464) calls clearTopicProgress, clearExamHistory, clearSimulationHistory, clearHazardHistory, clearBookmarks, clearWrongAnswers, clearStreak, and invalidateCaches. None of these sub-functions issue a safeWrite that removes the UserDefaults keys "study_activity" (ProgressStore+Activity.swift), "daily_challenge_history", "daily_challenge_last_date", or "daily_challenge_streak" (ProgressStore+Engagement.swift). invalidateCaches() (line 84-111) does nil the corresponding in-memory caches (_studyActivityCache, _dailyChallengeHistoryCache, _dailyChallengeStreakCache, _dailyChallengeLastDateLoaded, _dailyChallengeLastDateCache), but the lazy-read properties in both extension files immediately reload from defaults on the next access when their cache is nil (e.g., studyActivity line 10: `guard let data = defaults.data(forKey: Self.activityKey)`, dailyChallengeStreak line 38: `defaults.integer(forKey: EngagementKeys.dailyChallengeStreak)`). Because the four UserDefaults keys are never deleted, the first SwiftUI re-render after clearAllProgress() repopulates all four caches from the unchanged persisted data. A grep of the entire codebase confirms there is no other code path that removes these keys. The only call site (SettingsView.swift:369) is exactly the "Xoá tất cả dữ liệu" button described in the repro steps. The bug is real and the described symptom — heatmap and daily challenge streak/history showing old data after a full reset — will occur reliably.

title: Stale-read-after-clear race: safeWrite is async but cache is cleared synchronously, so the first read re-populates the cache from un-removed UserDefaults data
description: Every clear* function (clearExamHistory, clearSimulationHistory, clearHazardHistory, clearBookmarks, clearWrongAnswers, clearTopicProgress) enqueues the UserDefaults removal on the background writeQueue via safeWrite (async), then immediately clears the in-memory cache via invalidateCaches() or directly. Because ProgressStore is @Observable without @MainActor isolation, SwiftUI re-renders on the main thread in the same run loop — before writeQueue has executed the removal. When any computed var (e.g., examHistory, bookmarks, wrongAnswers, simulationHistory, topicProgress) is accessed in that re-render, its cache is nil so it falls through to read UserDefaults.data(forKey:), which still returns the OLD data (the safeWrite block has not run yet). The cache is then repopulated with stale data and stays stale because no further invalidation is triggered. The UI permanently displays old history/bookmarks/etc. until the next app launch or next write to that key.
repro: 1. Build and run. Accumulate exam history (take several mock exams). 2. Go to Settings, tap 'Xoá lịch sử thi thử' (Clear exam history). 3. Immediately navigate back to the Exam tab. The previous exam results still appear in the history list and stats (averageExamScore, bestExamScore, examCount) remain non-zero. Confirmed because ExamTab and ScoreTrendCard read progressStore.examHistory synchronously on the main thread in the same view update that fires after clearExamHistory() returns.

verdict.reasoning:
The bug is real and provable from the code. The sequence in clearExamHistory() (lines 482–488) is:
1. safeWrite { $0.removeObject(forKey: Keys.examHistory) } — async, enqueues the removal on the serial writeQueue but does NOT block.
2. invalidateCaches() — synchronous, sets _examHistoryCache = nil (not []).

Because invalidateCaches() sets _examHistoryCache = nil rather than [], the next read of the examHistory computed var (lines 156–164) falls through to SecureStorage.load(...) → defaults.data(forKey:). At that point the writeQueue block has not yet executed, so UserDefaults still holds the old encrypted data. SecureStorage.load decrypts it successfully and returns the full old history. The cache is then set to that stale value (_examHistoryCache = results) and no further invalidation is triggered.

The re-read happens in the same run loop cycle: ProgressStore is @Observable without @MainActor, performReset() runs on the main actor (it is called from a SwiftUI View body button action, which is main-actor-bound in Swift 6), and @Observable fires change notifications on the mutating thread (main). SwiftUI schedules a view update before yielding to the background queue, so the examHistory getter is accessed while UserDefaults is still populated.

There is no guard against this: invalidateCaches() does not set caches to their empty sentinel ([] / Set() / 0); it sets them to nil, which is precisely the "not yet loaded — please read from UserDefaults" state. The same pattern exists in clearSimulationHistory, clearHazardHistory, clearBookmarks, and clearWrongAnswers. clearTopicProgress avoids this specific form by directly zeroing _topicProgressCache.removeAll() (empty dict, not nil), so it is not affected. clearStreak also zeroes individual fields explicitly rather than delegating to invalidateCaches().

The fix would be: after safeWrite, set each affected cache field to its empty value ([] or Set()) instead of delegating to invalidateCaches() which sets them to nil. Alternatively, perform the UserDefaults removal synchronously (writeQueue.sync) before invalidating, or set the affected caches to empty sentinels before enqueueing the async write.

title: Task.detached scan in ensureStatsLoaded can overwrite post-clearCache state, corrupting cachedCount and cachedIds
description: ensureStatsLoaded() sets _statsLoaded = true and launches a Task.detached that scans the cache directory and then calls MainActor.run to write self.cachedIds, self.cachedCount, and self.cacheSizeMB. clearCache() deletes all files and synchronously zeroes these same properties. If the Task.detached scan finishes (and its MainActor.run continuation is queued) AFTER clearCache() has zeroed the properties, but BEFORE the MainActor.run continuation executes, the continuation runs last and writes the stale scan results (non-zero ids/count/size) back over the zeroed values. clearCache() also never resets _statsLoaded, so ensureStatsLoaded cannot be called again to re-scan. The net result: cachedIds contains IDs for files that no longer exist, cachedCount is non-zero, isCached may return true, and the UI shows incorrect cached video counts after clearing. Calls to localURL(for:) will return nil (correct, because the file-existence check is live), but the count metrics stay wrong for the lifetime of the session.
repro: 1. Cache several hazard videos. 2. Open any screen that calls ensureStatsLoaded() (e.g., the download panel), which starts the background scan. 3. Immediately navigate to Settings and tap 'Clear Cache' while the scan task is still running but has already finished its file-system loop and is about to execute MainActor.run. 4. After clearCache() returns, cachedCount may still show a non-zero value. Easier to reproduce on a slow device or with many cached files (100+ ms scan time).

verdict.reasoning:
The race condition is real and provable from the code. Here is the exact chain:

1. The class is @MainActor, so all property reads/writes are serialized on the main thread. That is correct.

2. ensureStatsLoaded() (lines 85-106):
   - Runs on the MainActor (the caller's context).
   - Sets _statsLoaded = true synchronously.
   - Launches Task.detached (line 90). A detached task has NO actor isolation — it runs on a cooperative thread pool, not the MainActor.
   - The detached task iterates over files (lines 92-96), then calls computeCacheSizeMB (line 98), then schedules a MainActor.run continuation (lines 99-104) that writes self.cachedIds, self.cachedCount, self.cacheSizeMB.

3. clearCache() (lines 262-272):
   - Runs on the MainActor.
   - Synchronously zeroes cachedIds, cachedCount, cacheSizeMB (lines 266-269).
   - Does NOT reset _statsLoaded (confirmed: no such line in the function).

4. The race window:
   - The detached task's file-system loop is executing on a background thread.
   - While it is running (or even after it has finished assembling `ids` and `sizeMB` but BEFORE the `await MainActor.run` continuation has been dequeued from the MainActor queue), clearCache() executes on the MainActor, zeroes all three properties, returns.
   - The MainActor then picks up the already-queued MainActor.run { ... } continuation from the detached task and executes it, writing the stale non-zero values back over the freshly zeroed state.

5. No guard prevents this: the MainActor.run closure uses `[weak self]` and `guard let self else { return }`, but `self` is still alive (it is injected into the SwiftUI environment), so the guard passes. There is no version/generation counter, no cancellation token, no flag checked inside the closure to detect that clearCache() has since run.

6. Consequence of _statsLoaded not being reset: after clearCache() returns, _statsLoaded is still true. A subsequent call to ensureStatsLoaded() returns immediately at the `guard !_statsLoaded` check (line 86) without re-scanning, so the stale non-zero values are never corrected for the rest of the session.

7. isCached (line 67) returns `cachedCount == totalCount`. If stale writes leave cachedCount equal to totalCount, isCached becomes true even though no files exist on disk. localURL(for:) does a live fileExists check and returns nil correctly, but all count-based UI (cachedCount, cacheSizeMB, isCached, cachedCount(forChapter:)) stays wrong.

No existing guard closes this window. The [weak self] guard only protects against deallocation. Swift actors serialize access within an actor, but they do NOT cancel or invalidate already-queued continuations when state changes; the MainActor.run block is an unconditional write once it dequeues. The race is a genuine TOCTOU on the three properties between a background-scanned snapshot and a synchronous clear.
